question: Does the image depict a physics force analysis diagram consisting of a block on an inclined plane connected to a suspended object via a pulley?
Is the inclined plane depicted at an angle of approximately 30° (or explicitly labeled as such)?
Are there separate free-body diagrams (FBDs) drawn for both the block (mass m) and the suspended object (mass M)?
Is a rectangular coordinate system visually established adjacent to the diagrams?
Are the axes of the coordinate system aligned along the incline direction and perpendicular to the incline?
Are the component forces (orthogonal decomposition) indicated by dashed lines?
Are the specific force labels 'mg', 'N', 'f', and 'T' present in the image?
In the block's diagram, is the gravitational force vector (mg) drawn pointing vertically downwards?
In the block's diagram, is the normal force vector (N) drawn perpendicular to the surface of the inclined plane?
In the block's diagram, is the frictional force vector (f) drawn parallel to the inclined plane?
In the block's diagram, is the tension force vector (T) drawn along the incline towards the pulley?
In the suspended object's diagram, are the gravitational force (Mg) pointing down and tension (T) pointing up correctly shown?
